A HANDFUL OF VIOLETS.

'Twas a morning in midwinter,
	Cold but clear as joyous May,
Over dale and lonely woodland,
	A snow-white mantle lay.
The delicate hoar frost decking
	Tree and shrub with strange device;
And a filmy mist was rising
	From the river's broken ice.
The sun shone out with a brightness
	That dazzled the watching eye;
The smoke curled lazily upward
	Towards the clear azure sky.
The wind rose high and sadly moaned
	Through the boughs of leafless trees,
And whirled the white snow into drifts,
	Joined the ice-clad river's breeze.
Thus it was when they came to me, 
	Still with dewy fragrance wet,
Plucked in the warm and sunny south,
	A "handful of violets."
And in fancy I saw the hands
	That gathered the blossoms rare;
A sad-faced mother, somber clad,
	And fatherless boy so fair.
So tenderly, aye reverently,
	She stooped the flowers to part,
With thoughts of their hoped-for mission
	Fast filling her aching heart.
The great brown eyes gaze wond'ringly
	At his mother's falling tears,
"Poor mamma," the sweet lips whisper,
	Words of comfort low she hears.
Two weeks, with water daily changed,
	They bloomed nor their beauty lost,
'Till sunshine stole the snow away,
	And spring rains destroyed the frost.
Long before their fragrance perished,
	Over his couch they were strewn;
In the lone, beautiful city,
	With its peaceful dead entomed.
Wooed to life by balmy breezes,
	With their wishes unexpressed,
Far from the sunny south they came,
	On a northern grave to rest.
Only one lived, and that—ah, well,
	I have told you where it rests;
How the stilled heart beneath must love,
	That "handful of violets."
May 3rd, 1893. HYACINTH.


LOVE’S QUESTIONINGS.

IN the valley of the Temple
	Where God's work in beauty gleam,
From the snow capped peak or meadow,
	Rose-blown hedge or rippling stream.

In the shadow of the Temple
	On the hill-side fair to see,
When thy gaze was all enraptured,
	Came sweet tender thoughts of me?

Did you think of nature's lover
	How her eyes would feast the while,
Viewing o'er each scene of beauty
	'Neath the charm of thy sweet smile?

In the music of the Temple
	Swelling sweet with prayer and praise,
Came a thought of joys unmeasured,
	That we knew in other days?

In the glory of the Temple
	Where the angels met with man,
Great and precious truths revealing
	Of the gospel's glorious plan.

When the Holy Ghost in power
	Filled thy soul with perfect peace,
Did you long for me that hour?
	Did your love for me increase?

To my spirit comes the answer,
	In sweet accents soft and low,
Giving me a blest assurance,
	All my trusting heart would know.
					LILLIE T. FREEZE.


OPINIONS ABOUT NAMES.

	THE Ladies' Home Journal  for May
contains a number of articles on letters
from prominent women on  the subject of a
married woman using the husband's name
or her own also after his death.
	Our opinion has been for some years that
it is better for all purposes that a woman
use her own name, also that girls be given
one name only and retain the father's name
in  connection  with that  of the   husband
after marriage, then one knows directly who
she  is, and   her identity is  preserved.
Where girls have double-names, for con-
venience, drop out one of the names (even
if it should be the name of the great grand-
mother) and retain the name of one's own
father which is a courtesy and  an honor to
him.   The son should always (in our hum-
ble opinion)   in   connection  with  the
name  given  him  at  birth  have also the
maiden name of his mother  which keeps
up the dignity of both families and makes
things equal as well as signifying the distinc-
tive lines to which the families belong.
	We give here the opinions of some very
celebrated women writers of diverse views
on this subject.

	I SHOULD say that the general usage in
this  matter,  somewhat  modified  by  the
various clubs and other associations among
women, would be sufficiently correct.   In
formal  society  a  lady  may, perhaps,  be
more conveniently  identified  by  her hus-
band's name, as Mrs. John Smith.
	In literary life, however, and in associa-
tions, the woman should be known by her

[Column 2]
own baptismal name, which is that naturally
and necessarily used in her correspondence.
				JULIA WARD HOWE.

	ALL that I can say on the subject is that,
personally, I prefer to see a married woman
keep her own name—as Mrs. Sarah Smith,
instead  of  Mrs.  John  Smith—but  that  I
recognize perfectly the social needs which
arise to make it more  convenient  that the
name of  the  husband  be  used  in  family
designation.
		ELIZABETH STUART PHELPS WARD.

	I CONFESS to entire ignorance as to the
most  acceptable  manner   of addressing a
married woman, nor do I know that there
is any recognized  code or rule applied to
this question; therefore, I can only give my
preferences and opinions.   I do  not like to
see a letter addressed  to a married woman
by her own first name.   The husband's full
name,  not his initials,  in  my  judgement,
should always be used.  This is  more  dig-
nified for the  wife, more respectful to  the
husband, and certainly more convenient for
all business purposes.   One thing is certain;
personally  I  always prefer  to  have letters
addressed to me as,
		MRS. HENRY WARD BEECHER

	ACCURATELY speaking I know little of
the subject of  marriage titles.   In England 
a married woman is always called  by  her
husband's name,  whether  her husband be
alive or dead.   For instance, if  I were liv-
ing in England or Scotland, I would be ad-
dressed as Mrs. Robert Barr as long as I re-
mained  unmarried  a  second  time.     Mr.
Beecher and Mr.  Miriam told  me that  in
America a  widow resumed her  Christian
name, and acting on their  advice I signed
myself as Mrs. Amelia E.  Barr. But if the
custom of the country permitted, I should
infinitely prefer to  be called by  my  hus-
band's name because, if I am no longer his
wife, I am  at  least  his  widow.     I  think
affectionate remembrance would desire to
keep the husband's name.   I know I would
gladly have done so.
				AMELIA E. BARR.

	I DO not know that I recognize any code
in this matter, because a  woman  who  has
given her name to the public while unmar-
ried cannot expect that public to  observe a
strict social code when she marries.   At the
same time, I like to have my  social letters,
and all home and domestic correspondence,
addressed to Mrs. Robert M.  Wilcox, as it
is to my husband that I owe my home, and
the social position of a respected and pro-
tected wife.
	I do not expect editors and publishers to
take this matter  into  consideration,  how-
ever, as it is my work, and not my  person-
ality, which has made  me  known to them.
I expect them to address  me  by the name
which has made me of interest to them as a
correspondent.
	But there is always a greater pleasure to
me in seeing a letter bearing my husband's
name than those  which come simply ad-
dressed to	ELLA WHEELER WILCOX.


THE question seems to me a matter of
very simple solution.  Looking on it as
custom founded on reasons of propriety,
convenience and natural sentiment have al-
ready settled it.  A man marries a woman
to give her, upon her own consent, the
place and title of "Mistress" of all of
which he is "Master," his honor, his home
his family and his name.  The two become
one, and one name is therefore becoming
for the two.  Upon this theory of things
long established, the custom founds itself
and is perpetuated; the man's name is the
wife's name, as it is to be his children's,
and it is that by which, in all their joint
life and interest, domestic and social, they
are known and recognized as a pair.
Neither relinquishes individuality; each
has a personal name still.  Mary Brown is
not John Brown, of all that concerns them
together.  If he is Doctor Brown she is not
"Mistress Doctor," but still simply Mrs.
Brown.  This is also the case if he is
Reverend, Right Reverend or His Ex-
cellency.
	If Mrs. John Brown transacts business,
signs a deed, makes a will; if she keeps a
shop or a school, or is herself professional;
if she should become known as an artist,
musician, author—she does al these things
personally as Mary Brown; "Mrs." only
because of her married estate and surname;
and it is needful that her personal signature
shall be used and shall represent her. Mr.
John Brown is not herein responsible.
	I think a true wife will like best to be
known, to her life's end, by the name sh
would wish most of all to honor.  If she
become a widow she will prefer to keep it,
as in memory and faith; only waiving it,
perhaps, when a son's wife comes to bear,
in her turn, the very same.  Then the
elder wife and mother may retire, in a
modest dignity, upon her own personality.
It used to be a beautiful courtesy, in such
cases, for the world outside to distinguish
her as "Madam" Brown.
ADELINE D. T. WHITNEY.

	I FEAR that I have not any definite
opinion on the subject.  My own reason
for using Mrs. Hodgson Burnett on my
cards was a very simple one, indeed.
There were several Mrs. Burnetts in Wash-
ington during my first winter here, and as
one or two had initials similar to Dr. Bur-
nett's, confusion arose on one or two oc-
casions.  A number of my friends sug-
gested that if my card bore my name, as
Mrs. Hodgson Burnett, they would know
who had called at such times as they were
absent from home, and they had only the
card to refer to.  My first name is never
used, except by newspapers.  I blush to
discover that I am never of the slightest
use in a discussion, my one ruling opinion
being that everything which is not a brutal
crime or a shining virtue is, more or less, a
matter of taste; and as my own taste is to
leave to every one else the entire right of
decision in matters pertaining to theirs, I
must, perforce, wear at times an air of be-
ing indefinite, even on such stray occasions
as when I am not.
	FRANCES HODGSON BURNETT.
	WHENEVER the conventionalities of so-
ciety are concerned I think the husband's
name should be used, and whenever it is a
personal or business matter the wife's
Christian name.  In the case of a widow
she has a right, in my opinion, to wear her

[Column 2]
husband’s name when there is no son bear-
ing the same name.
	HARRIET PRESCOTT SPOFFORD.
	
	IT seems preferable to use the appellation,
Mrs. John Brown, rather than Mrs. Mary
Brown, when, because of the husband's
prominence, explanations and misunder-
standings may be avoided.
	Not many decades ago it was woman's 
highest ambition to become a wife, and she
who had reached the age of thirty without
having attained this result became an ob-
ject of pity and reproach.  Many a girl has
given her hand in marriage, unaccompanied
by her heart, rather than incur the unpleas-
antness of advanced maidenhood.  To be
an "old maid," in that earlier day, was to
invite criticism and derision.  At one time
woman was a helpless creature dependent
upon the strength and chivalry of man.
She not only bestowed her hand and heart
upon her home-giver, but her very identity
was sacrificed upon the marriage altar.
There are many Hannah Janes who, like
the heroine of Locke's pathetic story,
learned too late that the "rubbing out" of
self brought unhappiness to the one they
hoped to benefit.  It was very appropriate
at that time, when woman was known only
as the wife of her husband, that her identity
become merged in his. Mrs. Dr. Jones
was never suspected of being a practicing
physician; she was only the wife of one.
	Higher education for woman, resulting in
a broader field of usefulness, is gradually
changing many long-established customs.
The wife now recognizes the fact that for
the security of home happiness she must
not only be the moral leader of husband
and sons, but intellectual companion as
well.  In the larger development of her
mental and physical powers she naturally
desires to set aside the props and shields of
former ages, and to bear the responsibility
of her opinions in her own name.
	I would advocate that woman retain her
maiden name through life, and at marriage
annex the name of her husband.  Why
should Mary Clark become lost to the
friends of her youth, except to the few who
are acquainted with the fact of her marriage
with James Howard, when Mary Clark
Howard would convey her girlhood’s iden-
tity as well as that of her married life?
	ROSE HARTWICK THORPE.

	My own preference is to be addressed
Mrs. Lyman Abbott whenever it is in any
sense public, and always on the back of a
letter.  I took my husband's name when I
married, and I prefer to be sheltered under
the whole of it so far as I can be.  Of
course, in signing my name, I ordinarily
use my legal signature.
	A. F. H. ABBOTT.
	A MARRIED woman should always be ad-
dressed, unless she prefers it otherwise, by
her husband's name.  I do not know of
any hard and fast rule in this matter; it is a
question of accepted form in America.  In
England the husband's given name is gen-
erally omitted where the address is clearly
understood.
	CONSTANCE CARY HARRISON.

	IDENTIFICATION OF SIGNATURE
	(By Frances E. Lvnigan.)
	IN these days of multitudinous correspon-
dence the necessity for some way of show-
ing in a woman's signature, whether she

[Column 3]
be maid, wife or widow, whether she be of
the married orunmaried estate, is realized
as never before.
	The mystery of the usual feminie sig-
nature when it is attached to a business
letter, leads almost invariably to embarass-
ment on the part of the answered.  No
woman likes to be addressed with the Quak-
erish simplicity of "Mary Brown" whether
she be matron or maid, nor if she be the
former does she wish to be addressed as the
latter.
	In proceeding to some opinion as to the
best course to be adopted, the thing which
must not be done should first be thoroughly
understood.  The vulgarity of the titular
signature "Mrs. Mary Brown," or "Miss
Susan Smith," affixed after a "yours very
truly" can only be excused by its evident
intent to be one of the solutions of the
problem.  This form is the one to be
avoided.  A fashion recommended by com-
mon sense,simplicity and good taste, is that
of placing "Miss," in brackets, a little to
the left of the name,as:
	Yours very truly,
	[Miss] Mary Smith.
	Its equivalent for the married woman is
found by writing below her signature, “Ad-
dress Mrs. John Smith,” as:
	Yours very truly,
	Mary Smith.
	Address Mrs. John Smith.
	The value of concerted action is un-
questioned, and the necessity for it in the
accomplishment of any given purpose quite
as evident, therefore, if this problem of
identification of signature is ever to be
solved, it can only be by united agreement
on the part of women to adopt for all time
some such form as the above in their busi-
ness correspondence.
	The growing fashion of giving girl-
children but one name, as "Helen" or
"Katherine," so that when, if they marry,
they may retain, with their new signature,
their full maiden name; is part of this
subject of identification of signature.
"Mollie Irene Brown" is not as euphonious
nor as sensible as "Mollie Garfield Brown"
or "Nellie Grant Sartoris."  This custom
has the further advantage of securing the
woman's immediate recognition not only as
her husband's wife but also as her father's
daughter.  Mrs. Brown or Mrs. Sartoris
signifies but little, but Mrs Garfield Brown
or Mrs. Grant Sartoris tells its own story.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EVERYWHERE the world speaks for unity, 
everywhere the race sighs for unity, every-
where the heart of man beats for unity.
—W. S. Rainsford. D. D. 
	THE ethnographic exhibit at the Chicago
Fair will be partly within the main building
and partly outdoors—the collections being
within and other features without. The 
American department will include speci-
mens of native tribes living their usual life
and engaged in their occupations; relief
maps of the most famous earthworks of the
Mississippi Valley; models of the mysteri-
ous structures of Yucatan and Central
America, with casts of the hieroglyphics;
Peruvian mummies; palaeolithic implements
and relics of the mound-builders, photo-
graphs of mounds and ruins from Alaska to 
Tierra del Fuego; illustrations of primitive
religions, games and folk lore; and numis-
matic, zoological, geographical and natural
history collections in general.		Popular
Science Monthly.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


MENU FOR A HAPPY DAY.

"TAKE a little dash of water cold,
	A little leaven of prayer,
A little bit of sunshine gold
	Disolved in morning air.

Add to your meal some merriment, 
	Add thought for kith and kin,
And then as a prime ingredient,
	A plenty of work thrown in.

But spice it all with the essence of love,
	And a little whiff of play;
Let a nice old book, and a glance of love
	Complete the well spent day.”
							SELECTED.


PROMISE YOU WILL LOVE ME ALWAYS.

		I will ask of you a promise
			Now while I am young and strong,
		’Tis that you will truly love me
			When the years have rolled along.
		When life’s cares and trials leave me
			With deep lines upon my brow,
		And my brown hair turns to silver, 
			Can your heart love then as now?

		Now I’m young and have life’s pleasures,
			Gay young friends to cheer each day,
		And your love will be much dearer,
			When the years have passed away.
		When these friends that now are near me
			With my youthful days have flown,
		Tell me, can you love me truly
			Then when I have older grown.
	
		If your love to me is precious
			When each day with pleasure rings
		Oh, how much my heart will need it
			When old age unto me clings.
		When my steps are slow and feeble
			Hushed the merry sound of glee,
		After years of life together 
			Will you, can you still love me?
 
		When I’ve gone beyond the river
			Left on earth all pain and care.
		And you too have passed the portals, 
			And we dwell together there,
		I will want your love in Heaven
			When our circle is complete;
		Promise now that you will love me
			When beyond this life we meet.
						CAROLINE B. PRATT.


[Column 1]
SISTER MINERVA W. SNOW
	HONORED.

	A VERY pleasant social gathering was
held in the Manti Temple on the 22nd of
March in honor of the seventy-first an-
niversary of the birth of Sister Minerva
W. Snow.
	Her sweet and gentle spirit and her 
integrity to duty has endeared her to all
who know her and especially to her co-
laborers in the Temple.  Desiring to show
our love and appreciation for her, a few of
us sisters planned a surprise for her and in
cousulting Presidents Lund and Maiben,
they invited us to have it in the Temple.
We gladly consented and the following is
Sister Snow's description of it, written to a
friend:
	"We had assembled as usual at the
Temple and Prest. Maiben, at the close of
his remarks in the morning service, ex-
pressed a wish to meet the workers in an
upper room, after the day's work was done.
At the appointed time, all the workers,
with a few intimate friends who had been
invited, assembled there. Of course care
was taken by friends, to keep me in the
proper place and I had no thought of what

[Column 2]
was going on, until some of the sisters came
and said Brother Maiben was waiting for
us.  Hurrying with them, imagine my sur-
prise when I entered to see so many seated
around the room, I began to feel that some-
thing was up, but was not certain until Bro.
Maiben arose and spoke very beautifully
and feelingly upon our associations and
Temple labors and announced that our
gathering was in honor of the seventy-first
anniversary of Sister Snow's birthday.
	He was followed by Andrew Thompson
and others after which President Maiben
called on me to speak.  I arose feeling
very humble and full for gratitude to my
brethren and sisters for the kindness ex-
tended to me on this occasion. The meet-
ing was clo ed by singing Doxology, after
which a few moments were spent in hand
shakings, congratulations and good wishes.
Supper was then announced and President
Maiben kindly invited us to go to the
dining room where a large table was spread
and beautifully decorated with fruit and
flowers and loaded down with the bounties
of the earth.  About forty persons were
seated around the table, and everything the
heart could wish spread before them.
Near the head of the table was a large
beautifully ornamented cake, prepared by
the ladies of the Temple and bearing the
inscription.  "M. W. Snow, aged seventy-
one.'  We enjoyed ourselves until about
dark, when all returned to our homes with 
blessings for each other n thankfulness to
our Heavenly Father.
	"I feel that great credit is due to Sisters'
C. A. Conover, Christina Willardson and
Nellie Jensen, the committee who instituted
the surprise and also to Brother John Whit-
beck for his generosity and able assistance.
Nor must we forget President Maiben, who
in his kind and friendly manner made the
occasion as pleasant as possible. President
Lund was called away to the city, which
we very much regretted, but he left his
blessings and approval for the day.  We
pray the blessings of heaven may rest upon
him, for we love, honor and respect him in
this holy Temple.  All present contributed
to the occasion.  About fifty persons were
present, many of them presented me with
beautiful tokens of their love and remem-
brance, helping to make it one of the fairest 
flowers in the garland of memory."
	Upon reading this correspondence, I felt
that it would be only proper to send it to
the EXPONENT,  believing that its many
readers would be pleased to know of the
honor conferred upon one so widely known
and dearly beloved as is Sister Minerva.
	C. A. CONOVER.


U. W. P. CLUB.

	A MEETING of the U. W. P. C. con-
vened in the new EXPONENT parlor April
29th, 1893, with a goodly number of the
members in attendence. Mrs. E. B. Wells
presided, and Mrs. Lucy Clark offered
prayer.
	The minutes of the March meeting were
read and the roll call was responded to by
choice sentiments or verses.
	Miss N. V. Davis gave an interesting
and instructive lecture on the different
kinds of verses, lucidly explaining the
construction of the different stanzas, and
aptly illustrating the same.
	Mrs. Lillie T. Freeze read an original
article, entitled, “What is Religion,”
written in the lady’s usual able manner.
Gladys Woodmansee sang a song, which


was followed by an interesting and humor-
ous verbal description of Samoan women
and their customs, given by Mrs. Mary
A. Freeze. Miss Mamie Freeze favored
the club with some very fine instrumental
music, which was much appreciated. The
President spoke regarding the U. W. P. C.
becoming associated with the Territorial
Federation of Clubs, read the constitution
of and a communication from the Federa-
tion, and explained some of the benefits to
be derived from the connection. It was
moved and seconded that we join the
Federation. Remarks were called for, and
the members expressed themselves as
being in favor of the connection, the
motion was carried.
	Article second of the By Laws was moved
by motion, and Mrs Lizzie Wilcox, Mrs.
Ida Haag, and Miss Mamie Conrad were
voted in as members of the U. W. P. C.
In connection with the Prest. Miss May
Preston was voted to act as delegate for the
club, at the convention to be held in
Chicago during the World’s Fair.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells spoke of her expected
absence at the time of our next meeting,
but hoped we would carry on the meeting
as usual. Miss Pearl Russell kindly
favored the club with a vocal selection, and
benediction was pronounced by Mrs. Phebe
C. Young.
	Program for next meeting: Article on
the Sixth Sense, Mrs. L. G. Richards;
recitation, Miss N. V. Davis; music Mrs.
Maggie Bassett.
	GLADYS WOODMANSEE, Secretary.


PRIMARY REPORT, COLONIA
		JUAREZ, MEXICO.
MRS. LOUIE FELT:
	DEAR SISTER—Perhaps a few items in
regard to the Primary Associations of the
Mexican Mission will be of interest to you.
There is an association in each of the four
settlements of this Mission, and, judging
from the reports at our recent conference
they are all in a flourishing condition.
	The Mission organization was effected at
our February conference with Sister Ettie
Teasdale, President; Sisters Victoria Pratt,
and Abbie Young, Counsellors.
	Two Primary Conferences have been
held since the organization. The last one
was held on the 26th of August, President
Ettie Teasdale presiding. A good spirit
prevailed and many excellent instructions
were given by the leading sisters; also
Apostle Teasdale and others of the leading
brethren. There was a very fair represen-
tation from all of the associations, each of
which assisted in rendering a programme
prepared for the occasion, consisting of
songs, recitations, etc. The children all
took their parts in a very pleasing manner.
	During conference the primaries held a
fair in the basement of the schoolhouse,
under the management of the president.
	There was a very fine display of Child-
ren’s work, consisting of straw braid, hats,
quilt-blocks, stockings and various kinds of
fancy work, as well as many specimens of
cookery done by the little girls. While the
boys had a very fair showing of fruits and
vegetables, also a few specimens of their
mechanical genius.
	The whole affair was a credit, considering
the short time they had for preparation and
the busy season of the year.
	Ever wishing for the advancement and
welfare of the Primaries throughout
Zion, I remain your sister in the Gospel.
		CAROLINE C. ROMNEY, SEC.

SALT LAKE CITY,  APRIL 15 and MAY 1, 1893.


TEMPLE DEDICATION.

	THE General Conference of the Church
of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints which
was held this year, commencing on Tuesday
the fourth of April, 1893, in the large
Tabernacle in this city at 10 o'clock a. m,
was one of the most important Conferences
ever held. President Wilford Woodruff
presided, which fact was very gratifying to
all who attended. People came from far
and near all over the country and from
various parts of the United States, Mexico
and Canada. President Woodruff's sermon
was very comforting, his kindly counsel
and tender manner of expressing himself
working a deep and lasting impression upon
all those who sat within the sound of his
voice.
	The two days of the Conference were
occupied by the Apostles and leading
authorities of the Church, much of the
teachings being in regard to the Temple
dedication and the preparations necessary
to enter in-to such a sacred and holy place.
	The dedication services commenced on
Thursday morning, April 6th, in the audi-
torium of the Salt Lake Temple forty years
from the day when the corner stone was
laid. The interest attached to this impor-
tant event has a greater and deeper sig-
nificance than appears from the outward
seeming.
	In fact no event of modern times is so
important, and fraught with such momen-
tous consequences to the world and its in-
habitants as the opening of this holy edifice
for the administration of ordinances that
pertain to the living and the dead, to the
past and the present, to the endowments
and covenants that unite families and kin-
dreds in bonds inseparable.
	By the Latter-day Saints great things are
anticipated in these places of worship, and
the revelations of heaven and ministration
of angels is a part of the expectation looked
forward to and firmly believed in.
	Therefore when the dedication of this
Temple, “the house of the Lord in the tops
of the mountains” actually transpired, it
did not seem strange that atthe same time
a tremendous wind should arise, con-
sidering that the Prince and power
of darkness is always opposed to the work
of the Lord. 
	The Bible and sacred records refer to the
Temples built in former days. Some of
them were of magnificent proportions and
beautiful, splendid and gorgeous even in
their appointments and surroundings. Solo-
man’s Temple is considered the most fin-

[column 2]
shed type of all, and yet we who live in
this day and age expect greater results to
follow the dedication of this Temple just
dedicated than any other ever erected to the
most High.
	The prayer offered by President Wood-
ruff on the occasion of the dedication was
the most inspirational and wonderful ex-
pression of power and intercession with the
heavens that we ever have heard. It com-
prised everything essential to the salvation
of the world, and no order or organization
was forgotten, but the blessing of God
earnestly invoked upon all. 
	The sermons and discourses by the First
Presidency partook in a great measure of
the nature of the prayer and were full of
charity, long suffering, forbearance, and
forgiveness. The spirit of love, the love of
the Gospel was apparent through all the 
services. The choir which consisted of three
hundred voices (the ladies all in white),
under the leadership of Prof. Evan Stephens,
rendered the most excellent music, but the
most striking feature of the dedicatory
exercises was the “Hosanna Shout,” three
times repeated by the entire congregation
who rose to their feet and remained stand-
ing during the time. This shout of Hos-
anna thrilled the hearts of the vast multi-
tude, and echoed grandly through the mag-
nificent building; so exultant and enrap-
tured were the saints in their rejoicing that
their faces beamed with gladness, and the
whole place seemed glorified and sanctified
in recognition of the consecration made on
that momentous and never-to-be-forgotten
occasion. The prayers of the people had
been answered, that their venerable  and
beloved President might live and preside at
the dedication of this Temple, the most
important one yet finished or ever begun in
this generation. This was an accomplished
fact and there and then it seemed to some
at least who witnessed the ceremonies, that
angels and celestial beings were there in
power though not visible to human sight.
	The day was one looked forward to,
finally and longingly by many thousands
who have already passed to “the other
side,” and their joys was as great perhaps
greater even than ours who are here, as
the increase of knowledge in a higher
state, which they have doubtless attained,
must exceed that of finite minds.
	The services of the Dedication continued
until April 24, thirty-one meetings had
been held at which the First Presidency
and Apostles with many other leading men
of the Church were regular in attendance.
The same order of exercises was observed
all the way through, and from first to last
the most intense interest prevailed.
	The Sunday School children passing
through the Temple and joining in the
“hosannahs” must have been a sight for
angels to gaze upon, and undoubtedly
myriads of them were present.
	The Latter-day Saints look forward
with anxious expectancy to the building of
the Temple so long talked of and prophe-
sied of in the center stake of Zion, many
have been the prophecies concerning it, and
their fulfillment is as firmly believed in as
though it were an accomplished fact.
	The spirit of charity has been very appar-
ent and its influence has been comforting
and encouraging to the people. President
Woodruff and those associated with him
have set an example for the Latter-day
Saints in this respect that should be wide
spreading in its effect through-out the
length and breadth of Zion.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE readers of our little paper will
doubtless be glad to know that a volume
has been prepared for the exhibit in the
Utah Building at the World’s Fair in
Chicago together with the books by Utah
women. It contains one copy of each vol-
ume for twenty years and all the copies of
Vol. 21, except April 1st, which was not
ready when the book was bound. It is
handsomely bound in red morocco with the
name in gold letters on the outside, and
will speak better than mere words for the
women of the Territory.
	THE Committee in silk called by Mrs.
Salisbury just before leaving Utah for the
World’s Fair are doing excellent work.
Mrs. Margaret A. Caine, the Secretary of
the Committee, has been both North and
South and devoted much time and been
very energetic to look up cocoons, and a
variety of articles as well as reeled and
spun silk etc. The exhibit of silk is likely
to be very good and quite elaborate from
present indications. Mrs. C. W. Burnett
the Chairman, is deeply interested in mak-
ing the affair a success.
	The ladies of the Salt Lake County As-
sociation really deserve credit for what they
have accomplished towards the Columbian
Exposition! The several World’s Fair
Clubs in the county have done very well
indeed and deserve great praise for their
efforts, and the Chairmen of Committees
who have had special work entrusted to
them have not only been diligent in collect-
ing and looking after the specimens of work,
but have done much at their own expense.
The means used has been from member-
ships to the Association and from lunches'
given by the ladies’ and their proportion,
from the proceeds of the Columbian ball.
Some of the work has been very expensive
but the management has been judicious, as
will be seen when the accounts are' all
handed in. The ladies have worked to-
gether in the Board and in Committees in
harmony, and have really become much
endeared to each other through the pleasant
association.
	The books by women include nine by
Sister Eliza R. Snow, three by Sister Han-
nah T. King, three by Augusta J. Crocheron,
others are Mary J. Tanner, Reba B. Pratt,
Cornelia Paddock, Jennie B. Frasieth, Mrs.
W. S. McCornick, translations from the
German, Mrs. Mary E. Almy, Emily B.
Spencer, Hannah Cornaby, and other Utah
women. Another edition of Sarah E.
Carmichael’s book of poems is to be issued
by the Ladies’ Literary Club of this City
and will be placed in the Utah Building.
	Songs and flowers of the Wasatch, the
book of poems mentioned in the paper be-
fore will soon be completed it is beautifully .
llustrated in water colors by Edna Wells
Sloan, and will be dedicated to Mrs.
Margaret Blaine Salisbury. The very
large book of photographs of Utah
architecture prepared by a committee of
ladies contains views of the oldest and
newest residences with many public build-
ings, some of the oldest and
newest residences with many public build-
ings, some of the oldest style and others
more modern—a large picture of the Salt
Lake City Temple. The book is very large and
weighs fifty pounds. Mrs. Rhoda B. Lip-,
man and Mrs. Lizzie K. Crismon worked
very diligently in this Committee, and they
are justly entitled to praise from those in-
terested in the Utah exhibit

[Column 1]
PASSING THOUGHTS

	THE TEMPLE—The great event has
come—it is passing. Daily thousands of
people pass within the sacred walls, gaze
with wonder and admiration, join with
faithful hearts in the grand inspiring
prayer, hearken to the soul-stirring music
of the choir and the interesting and pro-
phetic words of the Apostles, join in the
hosannahs to God and the Lamb and pass
down the granite steps, without the portals, 
scarcely realizing that hours have passed,
for so rapt are they in the events of the 
moment. During the week of the dedi-
cation I have gazed from my casement
windows towards the great edifice and 
watching the tall graceful towers, like
angels in the blue sky have lived over 
again those precious momentsspent within
the walls.
	Some say that places so beautiful, so
chaste, so sacred are fit dwellings for
angels and that their forms may be seen
and their voices may be heard, so almost 
divine is the feeling that prevades the soul
while enjoying the services in this holy
place.
	They are the angels of love and mercy,
of good will and charity, and in our hearts
alone do we feel their presence. This 
dedication is to the Saints the greatest event
for many years. How long we have
watched the building of the Temple and
as stone has been laid upon stone our faith
and prayers have been offered for the safe
and perfect completion of the building and
now that it is so handsomely completed
well may we feel proud and happy. The
Temple is so dear, so sacred to us all. How 
as a little child I remember the sight of the
great stones one at a time being hauled
along the streets by two yoke of oxen and 
we would all stand for them to pass with a
feeling of awe and reverence, and so in 
later years when the trains brought the
white stones by car loads I always stood 
and waited to see them pass with the same
feeling, again when visiting the quarries in

[Column 2]
the mountains I felt the same sensation,
and I am only one of thousands who have 
watched the rearing of those walls and
seemed to be a part of them, so much have
our thoughts dwelt upon and longed for
the day of completion.
	On the sixth of April, 1853, when the
corner stones were laid perhaps those dear
ones who took part in the services hoped
and believed to also take part in the dedi-
cation, but alas! how few are left, only three
of the apostles, one patriarch and one high
priest, some few of the choristers and 
musicians, can any of us of a later gene-
ration imagine the intense delight of them
at this time when they look back over
those forty years of temple building and 
now realize the consummation of their
hopes and prayers. The thought is beauti-
ful to me, it is almost heavenly, and I 
greatly enjoyed seeing the greeting of those
aged fathers and mothers within the blessed
walls, and marked the happiness in their
faces as they seemed to say, "it is finished,"
and as I watch them and think of them I 
feel to say God bless you and may you yet
live many years to enjoy that house your
labor and prayers and faith have built. How
well repaid the saints all feel when they
behold the elegance and grandeur of this
temple with its massive walls, its frescoed 
ceilings, its marbled and inlaid floors, its
elegantly wrought and carved founts and 
baths, its soft carpets, its dainty hangings, 
its art windows, its exquisite furnishings all
blending and combining making a Temple
perfect and complete beyond compare.

	MUSIC:—During the temple dedication
the people of Utah had a wonderful and 
beautiful revelation concerning the divine
art. Did any of us really know before the
talent of our musicians? From the morn-
ing of the first meeting, when the three
hundred trained voices, under the baton of
our admired Evan Stephens, peeled forth 
those beautiful anthems and hymns until
the close of the three weeks during which
not only the Salt Lake choir but also the 
choirs of Utah, Weber, Cache and Sanpete
Counties sang for us there was a continual
and delightful surprise. The capability of
the Utah choirs has long been known and 
commented upon and their renditions of
difficult music have been much admired,
not alone by our own people but by 
strangers who have visited us,and our choir
leaders have had many deserved compli-
ments but their fame as composers has not
been so widely known. Surely all who
listened to the divine and inspiring strains
during the conference will no longer doubt 
their ability to compose and will bestow
just praise on all. The words alone, so 
appropriate and sweet, the music tender yet
sublime and the most perfect rendition 
made a feast for the soul.
	There was not a little surprise afforded us,
when we learned that the beautiful words
of one of the anthems, was composed by 
Miss Domettie Smith, daughter of Prest.
Jos. F. Smith, a young woman so modest
of her talents that this is the first public
use she has made of them and what a 
glorious occasion it was; surely it was well
chosen.
	The concerts in the large tabernacle af-
forded much pleasure to our visitors as well
as ourselves. Being a people gathered
from all parts of the world it is very de-
lightful to listen now and then to the
national songs of our native lands,especially
when so enthusiastically sung by our little

[Column 3]
children. Our own dear song, “The Star-
spangle Banner," is always new,though we
heard it every hour and the heart beats a
response to every word like a martial ac-
companiment. If ever I lived in another
land and that land became my home I know
that the words and notes of that song would
bring the tears from my eyes,the sighs from
my heart, at the same time my soul would
throb with pride and joy at the thought of
that precious flag. Feeling all this I know
that others feel the same and it was a 
happy thought to have the national concerts
at this time for every Englishman loves
to listen to the strains of “Rule Brittania,”
the Irish weep at the thought of "The
Dear Little Shamrock,” the sturdy Scott is
ever proud of “Auld Lang Syne,” and so
do the Welsh, the Swede, the Dane and
Swiss, the German, the Italian and the
French love their native land and all that
calls forth sweet memories of it. Truly
asks the poet:
	Is there a man with soul so dead
	Who never to himself has said,
	This is my own, my native land.
	Of all the national airs the one most
widely known and sung in other countries,
not its own, is the song of France—"The
Marseillaise," and the history of this won-
derful hymn recently published in music
is so interesting that I shall glean the
principal points for the EXPONENT. 'On
the 20th, of April, 1792, France had de-
clared war on Austria and Prussia and a 
feeling of intense patriotism pervaded the
French empire. Four days later the may-
or of Strassburg gave a banquet and
among the guests was Claude Joseph
Rouget de Lisle, captain of engineers, a 
young man popular and some what noted as
an amateur poet and musician. The host 
asked the captain if he could not write a 
war song for the departing volunteers and 
Rouget de Lisle answered yes, and went 
home. That whole night he sat at his 
desk, and when the morning of April 25th
dawned the "Marseillaise" was finished.
It was the result of one night's work and 
he called it the war song of the army of the
Rhine. On that very day it was played by
a full orchestra on the market place in 
Strassburg, and the "Marseillaise" cele-
brated its first triumph. The northern
wing of the French army brought the song
with them to the seat of war and copies of
it were quickly scattered over the whole of
Alsace. In Paris it remained unknown for
some time. First it must pass through
Marseilles. In June, 1792, the council of
Marseilles had collected a number of volun-
teers who understood how to die and whose
moral worth was generally such as to
deserve death. These Marseilleans were
feasted by their fellow citizens on June 25.
A citizen by the name of Mireaux from
Montpelier, rendered Rouget de Lisle's
song, the enthusiasm it created was tre-
mendous and on the following day a leading 
journal of the city printed the song under 
the title, "The war song of the frontiers.'
	The soldiers of Marseilles who went to
Paris brought copies of the song with them
and sung it while entering Paris July 13th, 
and again in August while the Tuilleries
were stormed it was sung by the whole
populace. In Paris, where the history of 
the song was unknown, it was called 
'Hymn des Marseillaise;' this became its
final name. As 'La Marseillaise' it is not
alone the patriotic song of the French
Republic, but the song of liberty or rather
of revolution for the whole world. There


are various opinious about the origin of this
song and many believe it to have been
really of German origin, the latest researches
however would show it to have been taken
from an oratorio called Esther by Grison
and thus of French origin.”
	The Marseillaise is now a hundred years
old. It is sung in every land and grows in
popularity every year. It is one of those
things that can never die. 	
	CAMELIA.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NATIONAL RELIEF SOCIETY.

	THERE was a semi-monthly meeting of
the National Woman’s Relief Society held
at Social Hall on State street, in this
city, on Tuesday April 5th, at 7 o’clock in
the evening. President Zina D. H. Young
in the chair, Vice Presidents Jane S. Rich-
ards and Bathsheba W. Smith were present
with her in the stand, and Emmeline B.
Wells, secretary. There were of the board
of directors a sufficient number for the trans-
action of business and a goodly number of
members. The constitution and by-laws
were read by the secretary; also the min-
utes of the meeting held at the time of the
incorporation.
	Remarks relating to the business of the
meeting and the benefits that would be
derived through the incorporation were
made by President Mrs. Young, and
Vice-President Mrs. Richards.
	The first business was the filling of va-
cancies in the board of directors caused by
the death of Elizabeth Howard and Harriet
D. Bunting since October last.
	Mrs. Adeline S. Barber of Logan was
elected to fill Mrs. Howard’s place in the
board and Mrs. Harriet Y. Brown of Kanab
to fill the place of Mrs. Bunting.
	Fifteen new members were admitted by
vote, and several of the ladies present spoke
briefly upon matters of importance connect-
ted with the organization.
	On Wednesday evening the annual con-
ference of the Relief Society was held at
the same hour, 7 p. m., in the same place,
at which the president, Mrs. Zina D. H.
Young, presided. There was a very full
attendance, the house being crowded to
overflowing. Verbal reports were made by
presidents and representatives from many
parts of the Territory also from adjacent
states and territories and from Canada and
Mexico.
	Susan E. Martineau, the representative,
had come four thousand miles to attend the
meetings and conferences at headquaters;
other representatives had come from Canada,
New Mexico, Arizona, Colorado, Idaho,
Wyoming and other far distant places.
	In addition to reports, interesting
speeches were made by the president and
vice-presidents. The conference was con-
tinued on Thursday evening at the same 
place with a full attendance, and other
representatives made reports. Mrs. Burton,
of Arizona, told some very interesting
things concerning the Lamanites in Arizona,
among whom there seems to be great im-
provement.
	Mrs. M. A. Winters, of Pleasant Grove, 
made some explanations concerning a work
she was doing for those who had suffered
during the journeyings and persecutions
of the Saints.
	E. B. WELLS, Secretary.


MRS. EAST’S VISIT

	THE many friends of Sister Willmirth
East, President of the Relief Society of St.
Joseph Stake, will be glad to learn that she
arrived in Salt Lake City, April 1st, enjoy-
ing good health, and buoyant in spirits, and
finding her loved ones waiting with tear-
dimmed eyes and open arms to meet her.
She has enjoyed herself very much in the
meeting with dear old friends during the
conference, and with joy exceeding and full
of glory for the privilege of attending the
dedicatory services of the most holy and
beautiful Temple, in honor of the God of
srael, which is glorious beyond description.
She attended the general conference of the
Relief Societies, and gave a favorable report
of the labors of the sisters, and also atten-
ded a meeting of the officers of the Y. L.
M. I. A. held April 5th, and reported the
associations to be in a flourishing condition.
Said since the visit of President B. H.
Roberts, there had been a decided improve-
ment and have felt more of the spirit of the
Lord than for years past, reported the
health of the people of the St. Joseph Stake
to be better than it ever had been previously
since the valley was settled. She will visit
the central board and attend the conference
of the Primary Associations while she is in
Utah, and other meetings wherever she is
invited and able to go. Her friends and
especially the sisters are delighted to see
her and hear her welcome voice once more.


W. S. A. SALT LAKE CO.

	THE regular monthly meeting of the
Salt Lake Co. W. S. A. convened in the
13th Ward Assembly Rooms, April 18th,
1893. Pres. Nellie C. Taylor ln the chair.
Opened by singing, “New America.’’
Prayer by Vice Pres. Elizabeth M. Price,
Singing, “Help the Working Women.’’
Roll called and minutes of previous meeting
read, amended and accepted.
	Vice Pres. Elizabeth M. Price suggested
that each one express her ideas; she won-
dered if something could not be thought of
to make the meetings more interesting, and
draw people out. Spoke of visiting people
at their houses, found they did not under-
stand these subjects, thought if they were
explained, more interest would be taken.
Spoke upon the law of consent. Mary P.
Silver gave her experience in visiting peo-
ple, not very satisfactory. Susanna Water-
fall thought women could do a great deal
of good, asked the Lord to prosper this
understanding. Mary A. Irvine said she
was heart and soul in suffrage, wished more
would attend the meetings, thought people
had wrong ideas concerning it, if women
could vote we would not have so many
dens of infamy.
	Julia Druce like the rest was a true suf-
fragist, did all she could to help the cause in
talking with others, thought we should
gain each other’s confidence, and should
ntroduce these subjects in other meetings
and sociables. May Talmage was interes-
ted in suffiage, she had also visited around
soliciting names, thought the greatest point
to be gained was to get people to under-
stand what suffrage was, hoped we would
get the interest revived. Had always en-
joyed the meetings, they were for our own
benefit, thought the more we came the
more we would want to.u
	Ella W. Hyde thought if we were united
in trying to create an interest in the meet-


ings we could make them a success. We
should each try to bring a new member.
	Pres. Nellie C. Taylor said that woman
was her own enemy in speaking against suf-
frage, they did not understand it, we do
not want to hold the reins but we should be
true to each other and uphold women and
seek to elevate man kind, thought it a good
dea for each one to bring a new member.
Meeting stood adjourned until the third
Tuesday in May.
	ELLA W. HYDE, Sec. pro. tem.


OUR BOYS AND GIRLS.

	WHAT shall we do with our boys and
girls too, for that matter? is a question that
is agitating the minds of hundreds of par-
ents in Utah today. I mean the poorer
classes. We make every effort and sacrifice
to educate them to some extent, and when
they become fifteen or sixteen years of age,
we need their assistance and it is time that
they should commence to take a part in the
great battle of life. “For life is real, life is
earnest.” Our girls are not all fitted by
nature for household drudgery, though they
should understand house-keeping in all its
details. If we send them to Salt Lake City
to work at anything, our minds know no
peace, for there is every allurement and
fascination to lead them astray. Why
should one dollar lay idle, when there is so
much to be done? Why do not our capital-
ists invest? Why should we import our
gloves, cotton hosiery, ribbons, corsets,
underwear, and hundreds of other articles
too numerous to mention. May the time
speedily come when some enterprising, pub-
lic-spirited, magnanimous men will start
such industries in outside settlements to
afford employment for our young people
that we may keep them at home. We
have everything in Utah to make us self
sustaining, if Utah will work for Utah.
Suppose our capitalists do not amass thous-
ands of dollars, they will have the satis-
faction of knowing that their works will
live long after they have passed to that
bourne from which no traveler returns.
	E. H. L. MURRAY, S. L. Co.

	MISS HULDA LUNDIN, directress of Sloyd
in the public schools of Stockholm, is the
only woman sent officially by the Swedish
government to the World’s Fair. She will
make an exhibit there of the system. 
	AMONG the musical compositions on the
programme of the dedicatory exercises of
the Woman’s Building at the World’s Fair
are a dramatic overture by Miss Frances
Ellicott, of London, and the “Jubilate” for
mixed voices and orchestra, written for the
occasion by Mrs. H. H. A. Beach, of
Boston.
	MRS. MAUD HOWE ELLIOTT goes to
Chicago on business connected with the
publication of the book on the Woman’s
Building and its exhibits at the World’s
Fair, which she is now compiling and edit-
ing. It will contain an introduction by
Mrs. Palmer, president of the Board of
Lady Managers; a chapter on the building
and its decorations, by Mrs. Elliott; a
paper by Mrs. Julia Ward Howe on various
Associations of Women; one by Mrs. Ednah
D. Cheney on Education; one on Literature
by Mrs. Laura E. Richards; one on Women 
in Science by Mrs. Louise P. Hopkins, and
contributions by other writers on various
branches of decorative art. The volume
will be profusely illustrated.

who frequent it.

R.S. REPORTS.

JUAB STAKE.

    THE Quarterly Conference of Juab Stake
Relief Society was held at Nephi, March
19th, 1893. President Mary Pitchforth
presiding, after opening exercises, of sing-
ing and prayer, President Pitchforth address-
ed the conference, referred to the first
organizations in Nauvoo, fifty-one years
since, spoke of the circumstances of those
early days, felt thankful for these as-
sociations; spoke of  the circumstances of those
early days, felt thankful for these as-
sociations; spoke of the dedication of the
Temple, of the benefits and blessings
that we may derive therefrom, urged the
sisters to look into their hearts that there be
no feelings or obstacles in the way to pre-
vent them recieving a blessing.
    Sister Ann Wilhey represented the South
Ward of Nephi, the officers and teachers
were faithful and diligent in preforming
their duties, the poor, were well provided
for. Had a great desire to attend the dedica-
tion of the Temple.
    Counselors Amy L. Bigler expressed
thankfulness at being present, sickness pre-
venting her meeting with us for some time,
desired an interest in the prayers of the
society, that she may ever be faithful to the
cause, and do the will of her Heavenly
Father continually. Sister Rebecca Udall,
praised the energy and faithful labors of the
sisters of the North Ward of Nephi, her
faith in the Gospel had never faltered, desired
to be present at the dedication of the Tem-
ple of Temples.
    Counselors Hannah Jones, urged the
sisters to criticize their hearts that no wrong
feelings exist therein, bore a faithful testi-
mony to the religion we are engaged in.
    Sister Bessie Kendall and Sister Elizabeth
Miller rejoiced in the work, desired to live
firm and faithful.
    Mrs. Langley Bailey then read resolu-
tions of respect gotten up by the sisters of
the Juarez Relief Society, Mexico, in
memory of our beloved friend and Sister,
Matilda E. Teasdeal. 
    Bro. Charles Sperry felt that each as-
sociation were mutually interested together
for the same cause, desired those that
should go to the Temple to have their
lamps trimmed and burning, hoped none
that felt themselves unworthy would enter
that holy Temple, felt that the Spirit of
God was in our midst.
   Prest. Pitchforth spoke of the non repre-
sentations of the Juab, Levan and Eureka
branches, the unfavorable weather having
prevented their attendance, had recently
visited the branches,they were in a flourish-
ing condition, all seemed to enjoy the spirit
of their callings, felt to rejoice in the hum-
ble spirit of our meeting, prayed that the
Spirit of God might rest on all present.
    Bishop Warner felt that all who entered
the Temple would recieve a blessing there-
from, exhorted all to live up to their
priveleges. Sister Bessie Kendall was set
apart as first Counselor and Emma Cole as
second Counselor of the North Ward of
Nephi.
    The Spirit of God was poured out in
rich abundance upon the speakers and an
humble forgiving spirit prevailed with all
present. 
   Benediction vas pronounced by Patriarch
Udall. Conference adjourned for three
months.
			ELLEN L. GOLDSBROUGH.
				                Stake Sec. R. S.


SANPETE.

	THE Relief Society Conference of the
Sanpete Stake of Zion held in Ephraim,
March 11, 1893. Counselor Sarah Petersen
presiding. Singing, "O give me back my
Prophet dear." Prayer by Bro. R. N.
Allred. Singing, "Prayer is the soul's
sincere desire." Statistical and financial
report of the Relief Society of the Stake
read, also a letter from President M. A. P.
Hyde. The Relief Society of Mt. Pleasant,
Fairview, Fountain Green, Ephraim and
Fayette were each reported as being in a
flourishing condition. Counselor Minerva
W. Snow felt that we had been edified by
the sisters that had spoken. Said here be-
fore me are some of the children of our
Father in Heaven and those who are striv-
ing to keep the commandments of God.
Seek unto the Lord in humility. The
Relief Society was organized by the
Prophet Joseph. We are working with
men who hold the Holy Priesthood. We
are wonderfully blessed by having the
privilege of working in the Holy Temple.
Spoke at some length of her labors in the
Manti Temple. Advised us to study our
duties to the best of our ability and ask the
Lord to assist us. We should prepare our-
selves that we may be worthy of going in-
to the Salt Lake Temple. Let us be hum-
ble and prayerful continually that we may
be counted worthy of the blessings of our
Heavenly Father. If we see a sister that
seems to be on the downward track, let us
go to her and speak kindly and try to lead
her in the right path. She may have had
trials that have been hard to bear. Ex-
horted us to try and be more united and
strive to live lives of holiness.
	Apostle Lund expressed his pleasure in
meeting with the sisters in their conference.
Said, "We do not live this life only. A
greater object is in view, viz., eternal life."
	The good you do here is preparing you
for hereafter. In doing good you bless
yourself as well as your husband. Do not
speak of the evils of others but be kind and
charitable to all. Strive to help bear the
burdens of others and your own will be-
come lighter. Blessed the sisters in all
their endeavors to do good. Sister M. W.
Snow requested the sisters to remember
Sister M. A. P. Hyde in their prayers.
Counselor Sarah Peterson exhorted us to all
diligence in keeping the commandments of
God. Said we did not comprehend the day
in which we live, that we have the privilege
of going into the Temples.
	Sister Helen Armstrong was unanimousiy
sustained as the corresponding secretary of
the Stake. Conference adjourned for three
months. Choir sang, "We thank thee O,
God, for a Prophet." Benediction by Bro.
N. Christensen.
		MARY ANN FRANTZEN, Stake Sec.


BEAVER STAKE.
	THE quarterly Conference of the Relief
Society of this Stake was held in the L. D.
S. Meeting House, Beaver City, Aug. 6th,
1892.  President Lucinda Howd presiding.
	After the usual opening exercises the
local Presidents reported their respective
branches of the Relief Society as being in
good condition and doing all they could to
fulfill their mission.  The statistical and
financial reports were then read.  The bal-
ance of the time was mostly occupied by
Mrs. Elizabeth Howard and Mrs. Ardella
Eardly.  Such topics as:  keeping minutes
properly, punctuality, the necessity and


efficacy of prayer, cleanliness, taking the
EXPONENT,sustaining the Deseret Hospital;
and teaching our children obedience to the
priesthood and the law of tithing, Word of
Wisdom, observance of the Sabbath and
fast days, were treated by these sisters in a
very pleasing and instructive manner.  We
enjoyed the visit and their instructions very
much and hope they will come again soon.
	President C. D. White was pleased with
the counsel we had had.  Thought if it
were carried out our lives would be happier
and more useful.  The Stake officers were
unanimously sustained by the conference as
follows:  Lucinda Howd, Pres.  S. M.
Dell, 1st Coun.  Lydia White, 2nd Coun.
Louissa Jones, Sec.
		LOUISSA JONES, Stake Sec.


NOTES AND NEWS.
	MRS. FENWICK MILLER will attend the
World’s Congress of Representative Women
as an official Representative from the
Women’s Franchise League of England,
and will deliver an address upon “Wives
and Mothers: Their Civil Duties,” in the
Department Congress of the National
American Woman Suffrage Association.
	“SOME of our most celebrated women
are going over to Chicago for “The World’s
Fair.” Among them are Mrs. Fenwick
Miller, the distinguished authoress, journa-
list and lecturer. Mrs. Miller will doubtless
write an account of the Fair for some of the
London papers, and I am glad to hear that
she is also going to show the Americans
how an Englishwoman can speak. Among
our platform speakers Mrs. Miller is almost
supreme. She is never nervous, she has
a good presence, an excellent manner, and
a beautiful voice. And she has the subtle,
incommunicable art of being able to enchain
the attention of large crowds of people. I
believe she proposes to be absent for about
two months. I wonder if she will intro-
duce to Brother Johnathan her lecture on
London Society? I fancy it would prove
one of the most popular lectures ever de-
livered in America.”
	From the “Hearth and Home” of Eng-
land.
	NOT long since the Roman Church was
the only one to observe the Easter season,
but of late years the custom has spread all
over the Christian world and at Easter tide
the churches vie with each other in their
beautiful floral decorations, trailing vines
and potted plants are on every side, but the
flower most used for the Easter decoration
is the fair tall Easter lily. In Bermuda at
this time of the year there are vast fields of
these glorious lilies bowing and swaying in
the April winds. What a wonderful sight
this would be to us who so admire but rare-
ly see this graceful flower.
	'If I only could write the colors
		Of the lilacs towering plumes,
	And make you feel in a sentence
		The spell of its sweet perfumes.”
	APRIL—the month of hope the month of
promise. How we laugh in its sweet sun-
shine, how we weep it its soft rains. What
fanciful thoughts possess us as we watch the
purple glow in its twilight—thoughts of old
folk’s love— sweet memories of the past fill
our soul and with the setting sun tears fall
from eyes that but a moment since were
radiant with smiles.
How sweet the poet sung who said, “My
love is like an April day, all smiles all
tears.” It is the month dear to the heart,


so tender, so gentle, like a little child it
seems, and every bud on the trees, every
leaf, every blade of grass is tenderly
watched for, do they not refresh the sight
after the long cold barren winter. How
eagerly we listen for the first sweet notes of
the “meadow lark” and how gently we turn
the soil for the first sight of the fragrant
violet, then to rejoice when the dark green
leaves and fair purple blossoms of the stately
lilac bush present themselves. Dear, ten-
der April we welcome thee.

[Column 1]
		MISCELLANEOUS.
	NO wound can be so deep as the one
inflicted by a friend.
	BETTER three hours too soon than one
minute too late.—Shakspeare
	THE supreme fall of falls is this—the
doubt of one’s self.—Countess de Gasparin.
	It is our own vanity that makes the van-
ity of others intolerable to us.—Rochefou-
cauld.
	SLANDERS are like flies that pass over a
man’s good parts to light upon his sores.—
Rule of Life.
	THE love that has no form of expression
may be very deep and true, but it is practi-
cally useless.
	A WOMAN'S sympathy, instead of scorn,
should go out to one of her own sex who
has gone wrong. 
	IT will be an ill day when we are so en-
gaged in seeking lost sheep that we forget
the lambs.—Spurgeon.
	I SUPPOSE it’s a bit hard to think any-
thing’s over and gone in our lives; and
there’s a parting at the root of all our joy.—
George Eliot.
	INVIOLABLE fidelity, good humor, and
complacency of temper outlive all the
charms of a fine face and make the decays
of it invisible.—Tatler.
	CHARITY is a principle of prevailing love
to God and good will to men which effectu-
ally inclines one endued with it to glorify
God and to do good to others.—Gruden.
	SINCERITY is to speak as we think, be-
lieve as we pretend, act as we profess, per-
form as we promise, and really be what we
would seem and appear to be.—Rule of
Life.
	CHILDREN have always a sympathy in
the agitations of those connected with them;
always, especially, a sense of any trouble or
impending revolution, of whatever kind, in
domestic circumstances.—Hawthorne.
	THE noblest contribution which any man
can make for the benefit of posterity is that
of a good character; the richest bequest
which any man can leave to the youth of
his native land is that of a shining, spotless
reputation.
	ONE of the easiest things in the world is
to find fault, and in no place are there so
many opportunities for doing so as in the
home. There are so many little things
occuring daily in any good-sized family;
such as misplacing a garment, leaving a
door ajar, and a thousand and one trivial
things that readily provoke the fault-finder.
	Few people would be willing to answer
“present” to the roll call of fault-finders.
Indeed, so insidious is the ugly fault when
once formed that it blinds its victim to the

[Column 2]
﻿possibility of seeing his own faults. So
busy with the faults of others he has no
time to attend to his own.
	Like all elements of character, fault-find-
ing starts as a very little thing, and then
grows. The habit of criticising friends,
neighbors, people at church, the preacher—
all these are the beginning of the chronic
fault-finder’s education. A genuine, Chris-
tianized love for one’s fellow-men will soon
do away with all fault-finding.


MARY E. ERICKSEN.

	ONCE more we are called upon to record the
death of one of our young ladies, who died Nov.
24, 1892, Mary E. Ericksen. She was born at
Round Valley, Morgan County, September 16,
1874, and was at the time of her death eighteen
years two months and eight days old.
Desceased was a member of the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints and a teacher in the
Sunday School, she was the daughter of Michael
and Mary E. Ericksen; her death caused a cloud
of gloom to come over us; and at the Sunday
School resolutions of respect were adopted.
	Resolutions in memory of Mary E. Ericksen
who departed this life Nov. 24, 1892:
	Whereas, an Allwise Providence has in his wis-
dom and in a sudden and unexpected manner taken
from our midst by death our beloved sister and
col-aborer Miss Ericksen, and
	Whereas: we the members of the Willard
Sabbath school, while humbly submitting to the
will of Heaven feel it a duty to express our ap-
preciation of her labors and our sorrow at her
seemingly untimely death.
	Resolved I. that the faithfulness and energy
manifested by her in her labors in the Sunday
School, Y. L. M. I. A. Choir, and the Ward be
held in grateful remembrance.
	Resolved II that in her death this Sunday
School has been deprived of one of its faithful
teachers and the Ward of one of its promising.
members.
	Resolved III. that we share the heavy burden
of sorrow which weighs upon her relatives, and
extend to them our heartfelt sympathy in the sad
bereavement.
	Resolved IV. that these resolutions be kept in
the record of the Sunday School and that copies
be sent to her parents and to the county papers
for publication Jas J. Chandler, Ellen Ward and
Jane Hubbard, Committee.

		"We miss thee from our home dear,
			We miss thee from thy place,
		A shadow o’er our life is cast,
			We miss the sunshine of thy face.
		We miss thy kind and willing hand,
			Thy fond and earnest care,
		Our home is dark without thee
			We miss thee everywhere.

		Peaceful be thy silent slumber,
			Peaceful in thy grave so low,
		Thou no more will join our number, 
			Thou no more our sorrows know, 
		Yet again we hope to meet thee,
			When the day of life is fled;
		And in Heaven with joy to greet thee. 
			Where no farewell tears are shed.”
										M.


OBITUARY.

	DIED at Mill Creek Ward, April 11th, 1893,
Ann, wife of Patriarch Washington Lemmon,
daughter of John and Hannah Cook, born Dec.
29th, 1819, in Newthorpe, Nottinghamshire, Eng-
land. Emigrated to Utah in 1853. Resided in
Salt Lake City till the year 1860 then removed to
Mill Creek Ward where she resided until her
death.
	In Aug. 1877, she was chosen Counselor in the
R. S., June 24th, 1880. When the Primary As-
sociation was first organized in the ward, she was
elected President of the first District, the first
position she held until her demise, from the latter
by her request, she was honorably released May,
1890, on account of failing health, in each of
these positions, she was faithful in the perform-
ance of her duties.
	Sister Lemmon was very much interested in
Primary work, the children loved her and felt
grieved when she resigned.
	The Relief Society will feel the loss of this dear
friend,her counsel was ever fraught with wisdom
and encouragement, many times she has said,
there was nothing outside of Mormonism that


was of any interest to her, for therein was con-
tained everything that was pure and noble and
calculated to exaet the human family. At the
meeting held in March she was in her accustomed
place, but when the ladies met in April one month
later, and was informed that she had died that
morning, the deepest sympathy was felt by all
present.
	She embraced all the principles of the Gospel
as far as they were revealed to her, truly she
could say, I have finished the work Thou did’st set
me to do.
	Funeral services were held at the family resi-
dence, there was a large attendance of relatives
and friends to take part in the last sad rites of
the dear departed one.
	The remarks of those who spoke were very
consoling to the bereaved family.
	The remains were laid to rest in the ward
cemetery there to await the resurrection of the
just.
	The members of the Relief Society extend
their heart felt sympathy to relatives and friends.
There is consolation beyond the grave, the promise
of eternal life to those who love and serve the
Lord and obey His commandments.
								COUN.
